Radiation therapy

Radiation therapy is most commonly used to treat micro-

reaction. The cytological appearance of fine-needle aspi-
possible) or as a primary treatment for tumours that are not 
rates from trichofolliculomas varies depending on the

Radiation therapy is prescribed in doses measured in 
gray (Gy); one Gy equals one joule of radiation absorbed 
per kilogram of tissue. It may be prescribed in single doses 
or as a total dose split into several smaller doses, known 
as fractions. Rapidly growing neoplasms tend to be more 
susceptible to smaller doses of radiation delivered in a 
greater number of fractions, whereas tumours made up of 
slowly dividing cells tend to require radiation delivered as 
larger doses in fewer fractions. A type of radiation therapy 
being increasingly seen in veterinary medicine is stereo-
tactic radiation therapy, which uses specific instrumenta-
tion and software to deliver high doses of radiation only 
to the neoplasm while sparing adjacent tissues (LaRue 
Trichofolliculoma on the flank of a guinea pig.
and Gordon, 2020). Radiation therapy requires both spe- 19.2 (© Peter Fisher)
cialized equipment and highly trained personnel and is 
typically available only in tertiary referral institutions.
Interventional oncology
Interventional oncology encompasses a variety of proce-
dures that can directly affect tumours or instead increase 
patient comfort without having a direct impact on neo-
plasms. They usually employ a combination of advanced 
imaging and specialized instrumentation. Cryoablation 
uses repeated freeze–thaw cycles to destroy neoplastic 
tissue within a localized site. Embolization attempts to 
occlude the major blood supply to a tumour, using a variety 
of agents to block the blood vessels. Stents may be placed 
to palliate obstructions caused by tumours (e.g. placement 
of a urethral stent to prevent occlusion from a transitional 
cell carcinoma), and percutaneous drains may be placed 
to allow palliative drainage of accumulations of fluid asso-
ciated with a neoplasm (Culp, 2020). These and other 
Trichofolliculoma on the dorsum of a guinea pig. The 
techniques may be considered for guinea pigs, although  19.3 mass has ulcerated and become infected.
there are only a few reports of their use. (© Peter Fisher)

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Soft tissue sarcomas
Sarcomas are malignant neoplasms arising from mesen-
chymal tissue. Soft tissue sarcomas are a group of sarco-
mas that have a similar pathological appearance and 
clinical behaviour. Neoplasms considered to be soft tissue 
sarcomas include fibrosarcomas, liposarcomas, peripheral 
nerve sheath tumours, myxosarcomas and undifferentiated 
sarcomas. They have a variable gross appearance, with a 
soft to firm consistency and a solitary or nodular presence, 
and they may be fixed to deeper structures or freely mov-
able. In general, soft tissue sarcomas show a low rate of 
metastasis but can be very locally invasive. Soft tissue 
sarcomas are encountered occasionally in guinea pigs 
(Figure 19.6). One of the more common soft tissue sarco-
mas in guinea pigs appears to be the liposarcoma, a 
malignant neoplasm of adipose tissue (Figure 19.7). Lipo-
Cut section of a trichofolliculoma from a guinea pig  sarcomas may be more frequently located on the limbs 
19.4
showing the central cavity filled with keratinaceous debris. than elsewhere.
(© Peter Fisher) The approach to diagnosis of soft tissue sarcomas is 
similar to that for other dermatological neoplasms. FNA 
may not yield a diagnostic sample because these masses 
Intraoperative image of excision of the trichofolliculoma 
19.5
shown in Figure 19.3. Note the large skin defect created, 
necessitating tension-relieving closure techniques.
(© Peter Fisher)
region sampled. The central cystic portion is usually quite 
large, and fine-needle aspirates often demonstrate keratin 
or sebum, with or without inflammatory cells, blood, 
keratinocytes or basal cells. Surgical excision of these 
tumours with narrow margins is curative (Figure 19.5).
Soft tissue sarcoma on the hindlimb of a guinea pig.
The much less common trichoepithelioma is a benign  19.6 (© Andrew Bean)
hair follicle neoplasm. Its behaviour is indistinguishable 
from that of trichofolliculomas, and the primary difference 
is histological. Surgical excision with narrow margins is 
curative and the prognosis is excellent.
Lipomas
Lipomas are benign neoplasms of adipose tissue. They 
are the second most common dermatological neoplasm 
of guinea pigs, estimated in one study to account for 25% 
of cutaneous neoplasms (Kanfer and Reavill, 2013). They 
are most frequently encountered in regions with large 
amounts of fat, particularly the subcutaneous tissue of the 
thorax and around the mammary glands. Lipomas appear 
as solitary masses or multiple nodules, have a soft to firm 
consistency and well demarcated borders, and are often 
freely movable. They can vary in size from small to very 
large. FNA of lipomas yields small clusters of well differ-
entiated adipocytes. Cytological findings must be inter-
preted with caution if a poorly exfoliating malignant 
Liposarcoma on the shoulder of a guinea pig. Examina-
neoplasm is possible (e.g. a mammary tumour with sur- 19.7
tion of a biopsy sample also demonstrated concurrent 
rounding fat). Marginal surgical excision of lipomas is 
bacterial infection with cellulitis.
curative and the prognosis is excellent. (© Andrew Bean)

The diagnostic and therapeutic approach to these 
neoplasms should be considered similar to that applied in 
other exotic companion mammals (i.e. other rodents, rab-
bits, ferrets) or small animals (i.e. dogs and cats) until more

Chapter 19 • Neoplastic disease
good, although this may change if a malignant neoplasm 
is diagnosed.
Surgery is also used for the treatment of vaginal 
masses. The approach is dictated by the location and 
attachment of the mass. The vaginal orifice is dilated using 
a surgical retractor and the mass is exteriorized as much 
as possible. The mass is then excised using whichever 
approach is possible given its location and attachment; 
pedunculated masses may be ligated at the base of the 
stalk and excised. A conservative surgical approach is 
rational given the lack of reported malignant vaginal neo-
plasms, although the veterinary surgeon (veterinarian) 
should use their clinical judgement on a case-by-case 
basis. Given the rarity of malignant vaginal neoplasia, the 
prognosis is likely to be good.
Ovarian neoplasia
(a)
Ovarian neoplasia is relatively uncommon in guinea pigs, 
and the masses are usually benign. Ovarian cysts (Figure 
19.13) generally do not have a neoplastic origin, though 
some ovarian tumours may contain a cystic component 
(e.g. cystadenocarcinoma). The following neoplasms have 
been reported to arise from the ovaries of guinea pigs:
■  Granulosa cell tumour
■  Teratoma
■  Rete adenoma
■  Papillary adenoma
■  Cystadenocarcinoma
■  Metastatic neoplasia
■  Ovarian stromal sarcoma.
The reader is referred to Chapter 18 for further discus-
sion of the more common types of ovarian tumours, the 
(b) associated physical examination findings and diagnostic 
investigation.
Leiomyoma in the uterine body of a sow. (a) Intraopera-

The diagnostic investigation of suspected thyroid 
neoplasia should be thorough. Standard CBC, serum 
chemistry, urinalysis and thyroid hormone measurement 
should be performed. See Chapters 6 and 22 for further 
information about thyroid hormone testing in guinea pigs.
Imaging is an important component in the diagnostic 
investigation of thyroid neoplasia. If CT is performed, the 
study should use intravenous radiographic contrast and 
should include the thorax to check the lungs for metasta-
ses. Nuclear scintigraphy may also be used, although few 
veterinary hospitals are able to offer this modality. It is able 
to confirm the presence of thyroid tumours, ectopic thyroid 
tissue and metastases by assessing the uptake of tech-
Intraoperative image of two large cysts arising from the 
19.13 netium-99 by thyroid tissue.
